coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots mediated acrylamide biosensing
coredata.dc:creator: Aafria, Shatrughan
Sharma, Minakshi
coredata.dc:description: Acrylamide (AA) is an unwanted food processing contaminant produced during cooking at high temperatures through the Maillard reaction between asparagine and reducing sugars or reactive carbonyls. In humans, it causes significant neurotoxicity, genotoxicity, and possible carcinogenicity. AA is categorized as a ‘Group 2A’ carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC). Bread, biscuits, and potato crisps are examples of foods heavy in starch that contain significant quantities of AA. Consequently, the detrimental effects of AA on human health make determining its quantitative content a crucial matter. Here, by immobilizing Hemoglobin nanoparticles (HbNPs) onto graphene quantum dots (GQDs) decorated pencil graphite (PG) electrode, we have developed a highly sensitive electrochemical biosensor for the detection of AA. Using cyclic voltammetry (CV), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), the developed working electrode (HbNPs/GQDs/PG) was examined at different stages of its preparation. The ideal pH and temperature for the fabricated biosensor were 5.0 and 35 °C, respectively and the biosensor demonstrated an acceptable level of selectivity, sensitivity, and stability along with a broad linear range (10 nM to 120 nM) and a low detection limit of 2.70 nM. The suggested strategy made use of less costly raw materials to ensure that it should be both effective and environmentally friendly. The sensor was effectively used to measure the amounts of AA in potato crisp samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Acrylamide
Graphene quantum dots
Hemoglobin nanoparticles
Processed food items
Biosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrodeposition of GQDs onto PG electrode
Limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful to the Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR, India) for the awards of Senior Research Fellowships [File No.: 09/0382(12481)/2021-EMR-I] during this study.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic reaction involved in the functioning of AA biosensor.
(a) UV–visible spectrum, (b) zeta potential, (c) FTIR spectrum, and (d) SEM images of GQDs.

UV–visible spectra of Hb (black) v/s HbNPs (red), (b) TEM images of HbNPs, and (c) FTIR spectra of Hb v/s HbNPs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Cyclic voltammogram of bare PG electrode, black (a), GQDs/PG electrode, red (b), and HbNPs/GQDs/PG electrode, blue (c), in 5 mM [K3Fe(CN)6] prepared in 1.0 M KCl. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
SEM images of bare PG electrode (1), and after deposition with HbNPs/GQDs (2).
Impedance spectra of (a) bare electrode, (b) GQDS/PG electrode, and (c) HbNPs/GQDs/PG electrode in 5 mM K3Fe(CN)6:K4Fe(CN)6 (1:1).
Cyclic voltammogram of AA-Hb/GQDs/PG electrode in 0.2 M acetate buffer (pH 5.0) & scan rate of 20 mVs−1.
Effect of various optimization parameters (a) pH, (b) Temperature, and (c) Incubation time (d) AA concentration on biosensor response.
A comparative study of different Hb-based electrochemical biosensors for AA detection.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Indium tin oxide (ITO), Multi-walled carbon nanotube (MWCNT), gold nanoparticles (AuNPs)Screen printed glass electrode (SPGE), Boron-doped diamond (BDD).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Application of HbNPs/GQDs/PG biosensor for AA determination in different potato crisps samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: All authors have read the manuscript carefully and have no objection to the submission of this research article to the journal “Microchemical Journal.” All the authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots mediated acrylamide biosensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Zoology, Maharshi Dayanand University, Rohtak (124001), (Haryana), India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Zoology, Maharshi Dayanand University, Rohtak (124001), (Haryana), India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Acrylamide (AA) is an unwanted food processing contaminant produced during cooking at high temperatures through the Maillard reaction between asparagine and reducing sugars or reactive carbonyls. In humans, it causes significant neurotoxicity, genotoxicity, and possible carcinogenicity. AA is categorized as a ‘Group 2A’ carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC). Bread, biscuits, and potato crisps are examples of foods heavy in starch that contain significant quantities of AA. Consequently, the detrimental effects of AA on human health make determining its quantitative content a crucial matter. Here, by immobilizing Hemoglobin nanoparticles (HbNPs) onto graphene quantum dots (GQDs) decorated pencil graphite (PG) electrode, we have developed a highly sensitive electrochemical biosensor for the detection of AA. Using cyclic voltammetry (CV), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), the developed working electrode (HbNPs/GQDs/PG) was examined at different stages of its preparation. The ideal pH and temperature for the fabricated biosensor were 5.0 and 35 °C, respectively and the biosensor demonstrated an acceptable level of selectivity, sensitivity, and stability along with a broad linear range (10 nM to 120 nM) and a low detection limit of 2.70 nM. The suggested strategy made use of less costly raw materials to ensure that it should be both effective and environmentally friendly. The sensor was effectively used to measure the amounts of AA in potato crisp samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: AA (2-propenamide) is present in many foods that have undergone heat processing, such as baking or frying. It is a colorless, odorless, and low molecular weight organic molecule with high water solubility. Thermal processing may trigger several reactions that lead to the generation of both beneficial and harmful chemicals. One of these harmful contaminants produced by food processing is AA. It is produced by heating food products containing starch and amino acids at high temperatures between 120–180 °C. AA is mainly formed via the Strecker and acrolein pathways [1].

At a large scale, acrylonitrile is hydrolyzed catalytically to create AA. Polyacrylamide manufacturing, wastewater treatment, papermaking, and tertiary oil recovery are common industries that often employ this chemical [2].

After consumption, it is absorbed by the body and then carried to several important organs such as the heart, kidney, thymus, liver, etc [3]. The IARC categorized it as “probably carcinogenic to humans”. AA is neurotoxic and damages the central and peripheral nervous systems of humans and other animals when exposed for an extended period [4]. AA is known to produce neurotoxicity because it inhibits neurotransmitter transmission and kinesin-dependent axon transport [5]. AA's genotoxicity and carcinogenicity are caused by its conversion into the highly mutagenic glycidamide metabolite. AA causes harm to the body by forming an adduct with biomolecules like DNA and neural proteins and leads to their alterations [6,7]. Because of AA's negative effects on human health, it is crucial to determine its quantitative level.

Chromatography and mass spectroscopy are the conventional techniques used to detect AA [8,9]. However, due to low molecular weight, high polarity, and low volatility, it is challenging to detect AA using these classical methods. Meanwhile, these techniques have many drawbacks including high costs, complex sample preparation, protracted detection period, and high operational requirements [10]. Therefore, it is necessary to create technology that is cheap and can quickly and specifically identify AA in food items. The use of biosensing technology is becoming more prevalent across all important industries, including pharmaceuticals, healthcare, environment, and food industries due to their effectiveness, affordability, and accuracy of detection [11]. Biosensors are analytical tools made up of biological sensing components that may generate precise and focused analytical signals.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted tremendous attention due to their low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and good photostability. They provide intriguing characteristics to electrochemical biosensing because of their larger surface area, excellent biocompatibility, rapid synthesis, low cost, low toxicity, high solvent solubility, and plenty of edge sites for functionalization [12].

AA may be directly detected by seeking materials for electrode development having AA selectivity. One of the most well-known proteins and a great model substance is hemoglobin (Hb). It transports oxygen from the lungs to the tissues and is one of the most prevalent intracellular metalloproteins found in red blood cells. The bond formation between AA and the amine group of the valine in Hb leads to the AA-Hb adduct formation [13]. An oxidation peak of the semi-reversible Hb-Fe3+/Hb-Fe2+ reaction as an active center was found in the Hb when measurements were done by CV [14]. But when AA is present, Hb adduct compounds are created, affecting the accessibility of the active center of Hb to PG electrodes. As a result, the active center of heme experienced slower kinetics and a drop in the peak strength of the oxidation current [15]. However, the heme groups functioning as redox centers in the enhanced quaternary Hb structure are unexposed. The usage of HbNPs solves the issue of sluggish electron transport from Hb to the PG electrode [16].

Herein, we developed a Hb/GQDs-based electrochemical biosensor for AA detection in processed food items. By using citric acid as an organic precursor and pyrolyzing it, GQDs were created with less complex post-processing requirements. The developed biosensor consists of HbNPs/ GQDs decorated onto a Pencil graphite (PG) surface as a working electrode. To our knowledge, this study is the first report on GQD-based electrochemical detection of AA.

Glucose powder, deionized water, potassium ferricyanide, glutaraldehyde, potassium ferrocyanide, and phosphate buffer were purchased from Merck Pvt. Ltd, sulphuric acid (H2SO4), silica, acetic acid, potassium hexacyanoferrate (II) trihydrate (Carrez I), cysteamine dihydrochloride, zinc sulfate heptahydrate (Carrez II), acrylamide, human hemoglobin from Sigma-Aldrich, and pencil graphite electrode, and different food products were taken from the general stores.

Autolab, Eco Chemie Potentiostat has a three-electrode system having an auxiliary electrode (Pt), a reference electrode (Ag/AgCl), and a working electrode (Hb/GQDs/PG). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was done using a Thermo Scientific device available at Maharshi Dayanand University, Rohtak, India. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were performed on a commercial basis in AIIMS, New Delhi. UV–visible spectroscopy was carried out using a Shimadzu device (160 A, Maharshi Dayanand University, Rohtak, India).

GQDs were synthesized by pyrolysis of citric acid [17]. Six grams of citric acid monohydrate was added to a glass beaker, heated & melted at 60 degrees for 20–25 mins until a dark orange-colored solution was obtained. Then, 1.5 M NaOH solution was prepared and mixed dropwise with the above-synthesized citric acid solution. The pH was adjusted to 9. Ultimately, the prepared quantum dots were oven-dried.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrodeposition of GQDs onto PG electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Before use, the PG electrode surface (2 cm × 1 mm) was manually polished with the help of silica slurry, and washed with distilled water (DW). The PG electrode was then dipped into a solution (3:1 ratio) of concentrated H2SO4 and H2O2 for 5 mins and washed with DW. Sonication was performed to remove adsorbed impurities followed by two–three times washing with DW. 1 mL of GQDs were ultrasonicated for 30 mins, mixed with 0.2 M of EDC-NHS (0.5 mL each) solution, and kept for 1 h at room temperature. This suspension was then added to 1 M KCl (50 mL)and electrodeposition was carried out onto the PG electrode surface through CV in a Potentiostat device by applying 20 successive polymerization cycles from −1 to + 1 V at a scan rate of 20 mVs−1. The resulting GQDs decorated PG electrode was washed to remove unbound matter and stored at 4 °C before electrochemical measurements.

The desolvation approach was used to prepare HbNPs [16]. First, 8 mg of Hb powder was added to 4 ml of 0.2 M sodium acetate buffer (pH 5.0) to create the Hb solution. To this solution, ethanol (4 mL) was mixed at a dropping rate of 0.5 ml/min with constant mixing. After that, 2 ml of glutaraldehyde (2.5 %) was mixed to it with constant stirring for 24 h. By eliminating water, glutaraldehyde promotes HbNP aggregate formation by aiding in intermolecular crosslinking with the functional groups located on the Hb surface. Subsequently, cysteamine hydrochloride (0.12 g) was added to the above-prepared solution and stirred continuously for five to six hours. Cysteamine hydrochloride provides the thiol functional groups for HbNP immobilization. The resulting mixture was centrifuged for 10 mins at 1500 rpm, at 4 °C. The pellets were spread in 0.2 M sodium acetate buffer (pH 5.0) and stored at 4 °C.

GQDs/PG electrode was dipped into HbNPs solution (2 mL) (prepared in acetate buffer, pH 5.0) and placed for 24 hrs at room temperature for immobilization. Finally, the electrode (Hb/GQDs/PG) was cleaned three or four times with 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) and used as a working electrode. CV, EIS, and SEM were used to characterize the working electrode.

Potato crisp samples (10 g) were smashed using a pestle and mortar and dissolved into 100 mL of DW. This mixture was left for swelling for 1 h at room temperature and then heated at 60 °C for another 1 h with constant stirring. The mixture was then centrifuged at 4500 rpm for 10 min. Hexane (2 mL) was used to defat the supernatant. Two milliliters of Carrez I and Carrez II were added to purify the resulting aqueous solution. After filtering the supernatant, the current biosensor was used to determine the amount of AA in it.

The current biosensor's detection mechanism relies on the formation of a Hb-AA adduct (electroactive property of Hb changes in the presence of AA), which results in passivation and reduced electron flow from the electrodeposited Hb/GQDs to the PG electrode (Fig. 1 ). Consequently, as AA concentration increases, more adduct formation occurs, and current decreases in direct proportion to AA concentration.

UV–visible spectrum for GQD showed two distinctive absorbance peaks around 225 and 280 nm (Fig. 2 a). The first peak at 225 nm is compiled with C = C group π–π* transitions in polycyclic hydrocarbons. Conversely, the n–π* transitions in C = O bonds are responsible for the second band [18]. The zeta potential value for GQD showed a negative value of 20.4 (Fig. 2b) suggesting the high stability of quantum dots. The FTIR spectrum of GQDs (Fig. 2c) shows a peak around 3328.06 cm−1 ascribed to the O-H groups' stretching vibrations. While the peak at 1015.88 cm−1 is assigned to C-O stretching vibrations. The peak at 1641.54 cm−1 is attributed to the C = O bond stretching vibrations of the conjugated molecules. While the peaks at 1387. 71 cm−1, 1566.81 cm-1and 1111.27 cm−1 are responsible for the stretching vibrations of C-N, C = C and C-O-C bonds respectively. The presence of –OH and carbonyl functional groups on the surface of GQDs indicates that the surface of these quantum dots is partially oxidized. These functional groups also increase their solubility in aqueous media [19]. TEM image of GQDs showed spherical shape quantum dots (Fig. 2d) with an average size of 8–9 nm in diameter.

UV–visible spectra of HbNPs and native Hb were compared (Fig. 3 a). Native Hb showed an absorbance band at 410 nm, while HbNPs showed an absorbance peak at 280 nm, representing the absorption of aromatic amino acid of polypeptide chain following nanoparticles formation. The loss of soret band indicates HbNPs formation [16]. The average size of HbNPs recorded by TEM was between 5 to 50 nm (Fig. 3b), which enhanced the surface area Hb. The FTIR spectrum of HbNPs and native Hb were compared. The native Hb displayed peaks at 874.83, 1044.73, 1637.87, and 3283.75 cm−1, while for HbNPs the peaks were observed at 877.00, 1044.35, 1084.03, 1641.55, and 3279.69 cm−1(Fig. 3c). The distinctive peaks demonstrated the existence of different functional groups. A short peak at 874.83 cm−1 is assigned to stretching vibrations of the C-H group of the aromatic ring. The peak at 1044.73 cm−1, indicates an amine group’s presence. While the sharp peak at 1637.87 cm−1 represents the carbonyl group stretching vibrations. The wide band at 3283.75 cm−1 indicates hydroxyl group stretching, while the peak observed at 1085.41 cm−1 represents C = N stretching in HbNPs. A significant shift was seen in the FTIR spectra of HbNPs and native Hb at the −C = O, –OH, and –NH2 groups. This shift could potentially be attributed to the application of glutaraldehyde, cysteamine dihydrochloride, and ethanol during the synthesis of nanoparticles. These functional groups helped in the good coupling of HbNPs with the PG electrode.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed in a Potentiostat/ Galvanostat having a three-electrode system, reference electrode (Ag/AgCl), counter electrode (Pt), and Hb/GQDs/PG as working electrode from −1 to + 1 V in 50 mL of acetate buffer (0.2 M) (pH 5.0), containing 100 μl of 1.0 μM AA. Cleaning of the working electrode with CV in the potential range of −2.0 to + 2.0 V (versus Ag/AgCl) in 0.1 M NaClO4 was done to remove the AA-Hb adduct from the GQDs/PG surface. The maximum current was recorded at 0.33 V (Fig. 4d ) therefore, this potential was used as the working potential for the developed electrode and used for further investigations.
For AA sensing, the factors influencing the performance of the HbNPs/GQDs/PG sensor, such as incubation temperature, duration, pH, and substrate concentration, were tuned.

The pH was adjusted ranging from 4.5 to 8.0 using the following buffer (0.2 M) at intervals of pH 0.5, to obtain the ideal pH, sodium acetate, and sodium phosphate for pH ranges of 4.0–6.0 and 6.5–8.0, respectively. pH 5.0 was observed as an optimum pH (Fig. 5 a).
The experiment was performed at various temperatures from 20 °C –50 °C at intervals of 5 °C, to identify the ideal temperature. The biosensor showed an optimum response at 35 °C (Fig. 5b).

The CV of the biosensor without AA was recorded in 0.2 M acetate buffer (pH 5.0). The addition of AA resulted in a change in current as a function of incubation time. An increase in the incubation time from 0 to 40 min significantly decreases the peak current value. The maximum decrease in current is observed after 30 mins (Fig. 5c). Therefore, 30 min was determined to be the ideal incubation period.

The standard solutions were prepared in 0.2 M acetate buffer solution (pH 5.0) with various AA concentrations (10 nM to 200 nM). The biosensor response and AA concentration showed an inverse hyperbolic connection (Fig. 5d). The detection limit (LOD) was calculated.
The current biosensor's operating range was 10 nM to 120 nM; beyond that, the response remained consistent. The current v/s concentration of AA showed an inverse linear relationship.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ)
ce:section.ce:para:
Using the following formula, the LOD and LOQ were found to be 2.70 nM and 9.0 nM, respectively.
LOD = 3 S
m
- 1
S / N = 3
a n d L O Q = 10 S
m
- 1
S / N = 10

Sensitivity was determined by measuring a decrease in current by adding one unit of substrate concentration per unit area of the electrode. Here, sensitivity was determined by calculating the slope of the inverse linear curve.
Sensitivity = S l o p e / A c t i v e a r e a o f w o r k i n g e l e c t r o d e
Where, slope (m) = 0.0005 and
The working area of the PG electrode = 0.835 cm2.
Thus, the sensitivity of the constructed biosensor is 5.98 mAcm-2nM−1.

By introducing 5 nM, 10 nM, and 15 nM of AA to a buffer solution with an unknown AA concentration, analytical recovery for AA was also investigated.
% R e c o v e r y =
C
spiked
-
C
unspiked
/
C
addedofspiked
× 100
The current biosensor assessed analytical recoveries (at levels of 5, 10, and 15 nM of AA) of 94.4 %, 97.8 %, and 99.0 %, respectively. These results demonstrate the high accuracy and reliability of the present biosensor.

To test the repeatability and reproducibility of the sensor, the AA content in the potato crisps sample was measured five times in a single day (within the batch, n = 5), and then after storing the samples for a week at 4 °C (between batch, five times). The results were almost consistent. The coefficient of variation (CVs) for AA detection within and between batches was calculated using the formula CVs % = Standard deviation (SD)/ Mean value. CVs obtained were 0.018 % and 0.041 %, respectively. The results demonstrated that the developed sensor had a high degree of repeatability and reliability. This may be because HbNPs and GQDs are covalently immobilized on the PG electrode.

To confirm the working electrode's selectivity, the impact of interfering substances was investigated. Both, propionic acid, and acrylic acid (1.0 μM) having structural similarity with AA were examined in 0.2 M sodium acetate buffer (pH 5.0). None of them showed appreciable impact. Therefore, the biosensor is quite selective for AA detection.

The constructed HbNPs/GQDs/PG electrode should be cleaned twice or three times using phosphate buffer solution pH 7.0 to be reused. The experiment was run once a week to make sure the electrode remained stable. The sensor lost 15 % of its activity after 120 days of repetition. This demonstrated the good biocompatibility of GQDs. The current biosensor has greater storage stability than previously reported biosensors (Table 1 ). The working electrode was stored at 4 °C.

Using the CV approach, the biosensor successfully assessed the amount of AA in the potato crisp extract (Table 2 ). For this purpose, 30 mL of acetate buffer (pH 5.0) containing 2 mL of extract was added, and the mixture was then used for electrochemical analysis. To quantify AA, the electrode was submerged in the buffer solution. AA content was determined to range from 73.2 nM to 106.9 nM. These values are comparable with previously stated reports [15,16,25].

We created a unique GQDs-based electrochemical biosensor for AA detection in potato crisps. The biosensor consists of a PG electrode modified with GQDs and HbNPs. GQDs have received a lot of attention nowadays due to their magnificent properties in biosensing. They have good biocompatibility with biological molecules such as Hb. Citric acid as an organic precursor was used to prepare GQDs. These prepared quantum dots showed high solubility, low toxicity, and good photostability and did not require any complex post-processing treatments. The high surface area of GQDs increases the electrical conductivity of the electrode. Hb forms an adduct with the AA and is therefore used as a biomarker for specific AA determination. HbNPs in amperometric detection instead of native Hb resolves the issue of slow electron transfer to the heme center. Further, the covalent immobilization of HbNPs improves the analytical performance and stability of the biosensor. The biosensor displayed an optimum response at 35 °C temperature and pH 5.0, with a very low LOD of 2.70 nM and a vast linear range of 10–120 nM with high selectivity (no interference was found) and stability. Specific AA detection in various processed food items may be accomplished using this electrochemical sensor.
Shatrughan Aafria: Writing – original draft, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Minakshi Sharma: Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR, India) for the awards of Senior Research Fellowships [File No.: 09/0382(12481)/2021-EMR-I] during this study.
